name: Amma
description: Amma is the supreme creator god in Dogon mythology. A solitary, self-sufficient being, Amma existed before all else, residing in a cosmic egg of nothingness. The creation story begins with Amma's desire to create life. Amma first attempted to create beings through parthenogenesis, resulting in a flawed and rebellious jackal named Yurugu. Dissatisfied, Amma then created twins, Nommo, who were perfect androgynous beings representing balance and order. These Nommo descended to Earth, bringing culture and civilization to humanity. Amma is often depicted as a tiny grain of millet, symbolizing the potential for creation within the smallest of things. Amma's power lies in creation, order, and the maintenance of cosmic balance. While Amma is the ultimate creator, Amma remains distant from human affairs, and the Nommo act as intermediaries between the divine and the mortal realm. Amma is responsible for the creation of the sun, moon, stars, and the Earth itself. Amma's actions are not always easily understood by humans, emphasizing the deity's transcendence and unknowable nature. Amma represents the ultimate source of all existence and the foundation upon which the Dogon worldview is built. Amma is the ultimate origin of all things, both seen and unseen.
subtitle: The Supreme Creator God of the Dogon

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Universe
keyMyths.description: Amma, alone in the cosmic egg, creates the universe through a series of actions and transformations. This process involves the creation of the sun, moon, stars, and Earth.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Nommo
keyMyths.description: Amma creates the Nommo, perfect androgynous twins, who bring order and civilization to Earth. They are seen as intermediaries between Amma and humanity.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Yurugu
keyMyths.description: Amma's first attempt at creation results in Yurugu, a flawed and rebellious jackal. This highlights the importance of balance and order in Amma's creative process.

sources.author: Marcel Griaule
sources.description: A detailed account of Dogon cosmology and mythology based on conversations with a Dogon elder.
sources.text: A detailed account of Dogon cosmology and mythology based on conversations with a Dogon elder.

sources.author: Genevieve Calame-Griaule
sources.work: Ethnologie et langage: La parole chez les Dogon
sources.description: Explores the importance of language and symbolism in Dogon culture and mythology.
sources.text: Explores the importance of language and symbolism in Dogon culture and mythology.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food and drink are made to Amma, though more often indirectly through veneration of the Nommo and ancestors. The Dogon believe that these offerings sustain the spiritual world and maintain balance in the cosmos.

worshipPractices.name: Prayer
worshipPractices.description: Prayers are directed to Amma, typically for fertility, health, and prosperity. However, due to Amma's transcendent nature, direct interaction is rare, with the Nommo serving as intermediaries.

festivals.name: Sigi
festivals.description: The Sigi is a major Dogon festival that celebrates the renewal of the world and commemorates the ancestors. While not directly dedicated to Amma, it acknowledges the creation and the order established by the deity.

festivals.name: Dama
festivals.timing: Variable, held after a death in the community
festivals.description: The Dama is a complex ritual performed to guide the souls of the deceased to the afterlife. It indirectly honors Amma as the ultimate creator and source of all life and death, reinforcing the cyclical nature of existence.

cultural.modernLegacy: Amma's creation myth and cosmology continue to be central to Dogon identity and cultural practices. Despite increasing exposure to external influences, the Dogon maintain their traditional beliefs, with Amma remaining the supreme, if somewhat distant, creator figure. The Dogon cosmology has also gained attention in esoteric circles, often misinterpreted or appropriated, but its core significance endures within Dogon society.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the beginning, according to Dogon cosmology, there was only Amma, a solitary, self-sufficient being existing within a cosmic egg of nothingness. This primordial state represented pure potentiality, a void pregnant with the possibility of creation. Amma, through an act of will and self-fertilization, initiated the process of bringing forth the universe and all that it contains. This initial act of creation was not without its flaws. The first being born from Amma, Yurugu, was imperfect and rebellious, disrupting the intended harmony. Despite this setback, Amma persevered, ultimately giving birth to the Nommo twins, beings of perfect balance and order, who would play a crucial role in shaping the world and guiding humanity. Amma's birth is less about physical origin and more about the emanation of existence from a single, unified source.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The central myth involving Amma revolves around the creation of the universe and humanity. After the flawed creation of Yurugu, Amma created the Nommo twins, who descended to Earth in an ark, bringing with them the seeds of civilization. They taught humanity agriculture, weaving, and the proper ways to live in harmony with the cosmos. Another significant story involves Amma throwing clumps of earth into the void, which expanded to become the stars and planets. The Dogon believe that the Earth itself is a female entity, and Amma's interaction with the Earth resulted in the birth of the first humans. These myths underscore Amma's role as the ultimate architect of reality, a force that shapes and sustains all of existence, even if somewhat removed from direct involvement in daily human affairs. The stories also highlight the importance of balance and order in the universe, values embodied by the Nommo.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Amma is recognized as the supreme creator, direct worship is less common compared to the veneration of the Nommo and ancestors. Amma's transcendent nature positions the deity as a distant, almost abstract force. Instead, the Dogon focus their religious practices on intermediaries who are more directly involved in human affairs. Altars and shrines are typically dedicated to the Nommo, who are seen as the messengers and representatives of Amma. Offerings of food, drink, and symbolic objects are made to these intermediaries, with the understanding that they ultimately honor Amma. Prayers for fertility, health, and prosperity are also directed through the Nommo. The Hogon, the spiritual leader of a Dogon village, plays a crucial role in maintaining the connection between the community and the divine realm, ensuring that the traditions and rituals that honor Amma are properly observed.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Amma's legacy is deeply intertwined with the cultural identity of the Dogon people. The creation myths and cosmological beliefs continue to shape their worldview, influencing their art, social structure, and daily lives. The Dogon's intricate understanding of astronomy, particularly their knowledge of the Sirius star system, has fascinated scholars and sparked debate about the origins of their knowledge. While some external interpretations have been controversial, the core of Dogon beliefs about Amma and the cosmos remains a vital part of their cultural heritage. In the modern world, the Dogon face the challenges of preserving their traditions in the face of globalization and external influences. However, their commitment to their ancestral beliefs, including the central role of Amma, demonstrates the enduring power of their mythology.

shortDescription: Amma is the supreme creator god in Dogon mythology, responsible for creating the universe and all life from a cosmic egg of nothingness.
